skill.name: ticktick-cli
skill.description: Manage Dida365 and TickTick projects and tasks from a Python command-line interface.
skill.summary: Use a Python CLI to list, create, update, complete, and delete Dida365 tasks and projects.
skill.author: DCjanus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-severity matches, but most weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and external-command hits are false positives from field names, Markdown examples, and template strings. AI review confirmed a real high-risk issue: the bundled OAuth Worker returns access tokens in a browser JSON response and the CLI directs users to a maintainer-hosted authorization broker. The CLI also performs authenticated network requests and can read checklist JSON from local files, so publication should wait for token-handling and user-consent improvements.

high_findings.title: OAuth Access Token Exposed in Browser Response
high_findings.description: The OAuth Worker exchanges an authorization code for a token and returns the access token directly as JSON from the callback endpoint. This exposes account access tokens to browser history, logs, extensions, screenshots, and any party that can observe the callback response. This is a high-risk design issue, although I did not find evidence of confirmed malicious intent.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The callback extracts access_token from the token response and returns it in a JSON response. The semantic context confirms credential exposure, not only a pattern match.

high_findings.title: Maintainer-Hosted OAuth Broker Handles User Authorization
high_findings.description: The CLI directs users to a hardcoded Cloudflare Worker authorization URL. That service handles OAuth callback processing and token exchange for users, which centralizes trust in the skill author infrastructure. Users cannot easily verify retention, logging, or operational controls for that broker from the skill package alone.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded broker URL and worker callback flow are explicit. Risk depends on deployment practices, but the centralized credential handling is clear.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Requests Use a Configurable Base URL
medium_findings.description: The API client attaches the bearer token to every request, and the CLI allows the base URL to come from options or environment variables. This is useful for testing, but a malicious prompt or environment change could send the token to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bearer-token header and configurable base_url are directly visible. Exploitation requires unsafe configuration or prompt-mediated command execution.

medium_findings.title: Local File Contents Can Be Sent as Task Checklist Data
medium_findings.description: The --item-json option accepts an @path value and reads the referenced local file before sending parsed checklist data through the API. This is legitimate user-requested functionality, but it can disclose local file contents if an agent follows an untrusted instruction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The @path read behavior is explicit and feeds task creation or update. It is not malicious by itself, but it creates a clear prompt-driven disclosure path.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Task and Project Operations Lack Interactive Confirmation
medium_findings.description: Project and task delete commands call the API immediately after receiving identifiers. The skill documentation warns that deletion is dangerous, but the CLI does not add a confirmation prompt or dry-run safeguard.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The delete commands directly call delete_project and delete_task. This is intended functionality, but the absence of confirmation increases operational risk.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Matches Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external-command alerts point to Markdown inline command examples or a JavaScript template string, not shell execution. The skill does instruct agents to run a local CLI, so command use is expected and should remain visible as a risk factor.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown examples or a thrown JavaScript error using a template literal. I did not find Ruby-style shell backtick execution in these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Field-Name False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported many weak cryptography hits, but reviewed code and documentation show API field names such as id, projectId, desc, status, and token. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The high-volume alerts align with ordinary API schema field names in code and reference docs. Reviewed files did not contain cryptographic algorithms at the flagged locations.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text that claims system authority, asks reviewers to ignore instructions, or requests skipped analysis. No evidence found for prompt injection in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches, and manual review of SKILL.md and README.md did not show override language.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Returned to Client-Side Callback
dangerous_patterns.description: The worker returns access_token in a JSON response instead of storing it server-side or using a safer local device flow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The access token value is assigned and returned in the callback response. This is direct evidence of token exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Untrusted @path Input Reads Local Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI treats --item-json values beginning with @ as local file paths and reads them into task payload data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file read behavior is explicit. It is only dangerous when an agent follows untrusted path input or sends sensitive content by mistake.

content.user_title: Manage TickTick Tasks from the CLI
content.value_statement: Task management often needs repeatable commands instead of manual app clicks. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use a Python CLI to manage Dida365 projects and tasks.
actual_capabilities: Lists Dida365 projects for the authenticated account.
Gets project details, including tasks and kanban columns.
Creates and updates projects with name, color, order, view mode, and kind fields.
Creates, updates, completes, and deletes tasks by project and task identifiers.
Adds checklist items from repeated command flags or JSON input.
Prints either formatted tables or JSON output for agent parsing.
limitations: Requires a valid Dida365 or TickTick OAuth access token.
Uses a hardcoded hosted OAuth helper unless users provide tokens another way.
Does not implement task search or jq-style filtering yet.
Delete commands do not include an interactive confirmation step.

use_cases.title: Automate Personal Task Updates
use_cases.description: Create, complete, or reschedule Dida365 tasks from repeatable CLI commands.

use_cases.title: Sync Project Checklists
use_cases.description: Create tasks with checklist items from structured JSON or repeated item flags.

use_cases.title: Let Agents Inspect Task State
use_cases.description: Return project and task data as JSON so coding agents can reason about current work.

prompt_templates.title: List My Projects
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the TickTick CLI skill to list my projects in JSON format. Do not modify anything.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a task named "Prepare weekly review" in project PROJECT_ID with a due date tomorrow. Show the created task summary.

prompt_templates.title: Update Checklist Items
prompt_templates.prompt: Update task TASK_ID in project PROJECT_ID with checklist items for outline, draft, review, and publish. Show the final task fields.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Before Deleting
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch task TASK_ID from project PROJECT_ID, summarize what will be deleted, and wait for explicit confirmation before running delete.

output_examples.input: List all projects for the authenticated account.
output_examples.output: A concise project list with each project name, identifier, color, view mode, and closed status.

output_examples.input: Create a task with three checklist items.
output_examples.output: A task summary showing the new task title, project identifier, due date, priority, and checklist item titles.

output_examples.input: Complete a task after confirming the project and task identifiers.
output_examples.output: A short success message and the identifiers used for the completed task.

best_practices: Provide tokens through environment variables instead of pasting them into prompts.
Use JSON output when another agent or script needs to parse results.
Fetch and confirm task identifiers before running update, complete, or delete commands.
anti_patterns: Do not send OAuth tokens, secrets, or private files in chat prompts.
Do not use @path checklist input unless the file contents are safe to upload.
Do not run delete commands without a separate confirmation step.

faq.question: Does this skill need network access?
faq.answer: Yes. It calls the Dida365 Open API and may use an OAuth helper URL for authorization.

faq.question: Can it work without an OAuth token?
faq.answer: No. The CLI requires an access token from --token or the TICKTICK_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: Can it delete tasks and projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes delete commands, so users should confirm identifiers before running them.

faq.question: Does it support JSON output?
faq.answer: Yes. The --json flag prints API results in JSON for scripts and agents.

faq.question: Can checklist items come from a file?
faq.answer: Yes. --item-json can read @path files, but only use files intended for upload.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish as reviewed?
faq.answer: Not yet. The OAuth helper exposes access tokens in a browser response and needs safer handling.
